questions.text: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded the Brahmo Samaj in:
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded the Brahmo Sabha in 1828, which later came to be known as Brahmo Samaj ('Society of God'). It was a monotheistic reform organization that opposed idol worship, caste discrimination, and the practice of Sati, drawing its theological basis from the Upanishads. Roy had founded an earlier organization, the Atmiya Sabha, in 1815 — but that was a predecessor discussion circle, not the Brahmo Samaj. The year 1828 is the correct founding date and appears in nearly every SSC CGL syllabus covering 19th-century reform movements.
questions.source: SSC CGL — founding year of Brahmo Samaj (1828) by Ram Mohan Roy is one of the most consistently tested dates in socio-religious reform questions

questions.text: The Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of 1856 was passed largely due to the efforts of which social reformer?
options: Raja Ram Mohan Roy
Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar
Keshab Chandra Sen
Debendranath Tagore
questions.explanation: The Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act (1856) was passed primarily due to the persistent campaign of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar (1820-1891). Vidyasagar made the legal and scriptural case for widow remarriage by citing the Parasara Smriti — an ancient Hindu text that permitted remarriage of widows. He submitted two petitions with thousands of signatures to the Bengal government and the British government. The Act was passed during Lord Dalhousie's tenure. While Ram Mohan Roy had earlier advocated for widow remarriage (before his death in 1833), it was Vidyasagar who directly secured the legislation. This distinction (Roy advocated; Vidyasagar legislated) is an important exam trap.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act (1856) and Vidyasagar is a standard question; the Roy vs. Vidyasagar trap is very common

questions.text: The Satyashodhak Samaj (Truth-Seekers' Society), which fought against caste oppression and Brahmin domination in Maharashtra, was founded by:
options: M.G. Ranade
Jyotirao Phule
R.G. Bhandarkar
Atmaram Pandurang
questions.explanation: The Satyashodhak Samaj ('Truth-Seekers' Society') was founded by Jyotirao (Jotiba) Phule in 1873 in Pune. Phule, from the low-caste Mali (gardener) community, built an organization that challenged Brahmin religious authority, performed weddings without Brahmin priests, promoted rationalism, and worked for the rights of lower castes and women. He published 'Gulamgiri' (Slavery) in 1873, drawing parallels between caste oppression and race-based slavery in America. M.G. Ranade and R.G. Bhandarkar were leaders of the Prarthana Samaj (founded 1867 by Atmaram Pandurang) — a different Bombay reform organization with a more moderate approach.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Satyashodhak Samaj and Jyotirao Phule is a standard match-the-organization question; commonly confused with Prarthana Samaj

questions.text: Which of the following is correctly matched?
options: Brahmo Samaj — Bombay — 1867; Prarthana Samaj — Bengal — 1828
Brahmo Samaj — Bengal — 1828; Prarthana Samaj — Bombay — 1867
Brahmo Samaj — Madras — 1820; Prarthana Samaj — Punjab — 1875
Brahmo Samaj — Bengal — 1843; Prarthana Samaj — Bombay — 1851
questions.explanation: Brahmo Samaj was founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in Bengal (Calcutta) in 1828. Prarthana Samaj was founded by Atmaram Pandurang in Bombay in 1867, inspired by Keshab Chandra Sen's visit to Bombay. The year 1843 (option D) refers to when Debendranath Tagore revived and restructured the Brahmo Samaj — not its founding. The year 1851 (option D) refers to the Rahnumai Mazdayasnan Sabha (Parsi reform organization), not the Prarthana Samaj. The Bengal-1828 and Bombay-1867 pairing is the most tested format for distinguishing these two organizations.
questions.source: SSC CGL — matching Brahmo Samaj to Bengal/1828 and Prarthana Samaj to Bombay/1867 is one of the most common 'distinguish the reform societies' questions

questions.text: The Brahmo Samaj underwent two major splits. What triggered the second split in 1878?
options: Debendranath Tagore's insistence on retaining Sanskrit scriptures caused Keshab's progressive faction to leave

Keshab Chandra Sen arranged his underage daughter's marriage to the Maharaja of Cooch Behar, violating the Samaj's own principles against child marriage; disgusted reformers formed Sadharan Brahmo Samaj
The British government pressured Brahmo Samaj to accept Christian doctrine; refusal caused internal split
Keshab converted to Christianity, causing orthodox Brahmo members to form Adi Brahmo Samaj in protest
questions.explanation: The second split in the Brahmo Samaj came in 1878 when Keshab Chandra Sen — who had been a vigorous campaigner against child marriage — arranged the marriage of his underage daughter Suniti Devi to the teenage Maharaja of Cooch Behar. This directly contradicted the Brahmo Samaj's founding principles on child marriage. Members who were outraged by this hypocrisy broke away to form the Sadharan Brahmo Samaj (1878) — a more democratically organized, genuinely reformist body. Keshab continued with his own 'Nava Vidhan' (New Dispensation), which increasingly blended Hinduism, Christianity, and Islam. The first split (1866) was about pace of reform: Debendranath Tagore's Adi Brahmo Samaj vs. Keshab's Brahmo Samaj of India.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the two Brahmo Samaj splits are often merged or confused; the specific trigger for the 1878 split (Cooch Behar marriage) is a detail tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: H.L.V. Derozio and Raja Ram Mohan Roy were both reformers in Bengal in the early 19th century, but their approaches were fundamentally different. What was the key distinction?
options: Derozio worked through the Brahmo Samaj; Roy worked independently through the Young Bengal Movement

Roy sought to purify Hinduism from within by returning to Upanishadic monotheism; Derozio encouraged radical rationalism and scepticism that questioned all religious traditions without proposing an alternative
Derozio focused only on women's rights; Roy focused only on caste abolition
Roy was a Christian convert; Derozio was an orthodox Hindu who opposed Roy's advocacy for English education
questions.explanation: Ram Mohan Roy and Derozio represent two distinct approaches: Roy was a constructive reformer — he found rationalist, monotheistic elements within Hindu tradition (especially the Upanishads) and built a positive alternative (Brahmo Samaj). He wanted to reform Hinduism, not abandon it. Derozio, by contrast, promoted radical rationalism and scepticism — his followers questioned everything including religion itself, without necessarily proposing a replacement spiritual framework. Derozians were provocateurs (eating beef, drinking wine publicly), while Roy was a thoughtful reformer. Roy's approach created lasting institutions; Derozio's movement flamed brightly and briefly (he died at 22) without organizational continuity.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because comparing Roy and Derozio requires distinguishing constructive reform from radical scepticism; appears in analytical/essay questions

questions.text: Jyotirao Phule dedicated his book 'Gulamgiri' (1873) to the American antislavery movement. What does this tell us about his ideological framework?
options: Phule was an American-educated intellectual who had personally participated in the US Civil War

Phule drew an explicit parallel between racial slavery in America and caste oppression in India — both were systems of dehumanisation maintained by dominant groups; the African-American struggle for freedom inspired his own fight against Brahminism
The dedication was purely ceremonial — Phule simply admired American democracy without seeing any connection to Indian caste
Phule supported the British because they had abolished slavery; he saw British rule as more just than Brahminic rule
questions.explanation: Phule's dedication of 'Gulamgiri' to the 'good people of the United States as a token of admiration for their sublime, disinterested and self-sacrificing devotion in the cause of Negro Slavery' was deliberate and ideological. He saw an explicit structural parallel: just as African-Americans were enslaved and dehumanised by white slaveholders who justified it with religion and claims of racial hierarchy, low-caste Indians were oppressed and dehumanised by Brahmin elites who justified it with the Vedas and claims of ritual hierarchy. Both systems were maintained through violence, denied education to the oppressed, and were sustained by religious authority. This global, comparative framing made Phule's critique uniquely powerful — it placed caste oppression within a universal framework of human rights rather than limiting it to internal Hindu reform.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the Gulamgiri dedication and its significance is a source-analysis question that appears in higher-difficulty papers; the American slavery parallel is the key analytical point

questions.text: Savitribai Phule is called India's first woman teacher. What was the specific obstacle she faced while walking to school, and what does it reveal about the resistance to social reform?
options: She was denied entry to the school building by the district magistrate; she had to teach outdoors
Orthodox opponents pelted her with stones and dung while she walked to school; she carried a spare sari to change into when she arrived
The British government banned women from teaching in Pune; she continued in secret until it was legalized in 1858
Brahmin priests placed a formal curse (shaap) on the school, causing parents to withdraw children
questions.explanation: Savitribai Phule (1831-1897), wife of Jyotirao Phule, taught at the girls' school they founded in Pune (1848). She faced violent opposition from orthodox Hindus who believed women (especially low-caste women teaching all castes) should not be educated or educate others. Opponents threw stones, dung, and mud at her while she walked to school. Her practical response — carrying a spare sari to change into when she arrived — is a vivid illustration both of the scale of opposition and of her personal resilience. She continued despite this for years. Savitribai is celebrated today as a pioneer of women's education; her birthday (3 January) is observed as Teachers' Day in some states. She also ran an infant home and worked during plague epidemic relief.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Savitribai Phule appears in basic facts (first woman teacher) but the specific obstacle (stones and dung, spare sari) is a detail that appears in more thorough questions

questions.text: Debendranath Tagore is called 'Maharshi.' He is the father of which famous person, and what was his major contribution to the Brahmo Samaj?
options: Father of Abanindranath Tagore; introduced English education into Brahmo Samaj curriculum
Father of Rabindranath Tagore; revived and organized the Brahmo Samaj (1843) and gave it the Brahmo Dharma scripture
Father of Dwarkanath Tagore; founded the Adi Brahmo Samaj after the 1866 split
Father of Satyendranath Tagore; introduced the concept of separate electorates for Brahmo members
questions.explanation: Debendranath Tagore (1817-1905) is called 'Maharshi' (Great Sage) and was the father of Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore. After Ram Mohan Roy's death (1833), the Brahmo Samaj lost momentum. Debendranath revived and restructured it in 1843, giving it organizational life and a scriptural basis — the Brahmo Dharma text. He made the Samaj more systematic and spiritually grounded, though more conservative than Roy's original vision. When Keshab Chandra Sen's progressive faction grew too radical for him, the 1866 split resulted in Debendranath's faction retaining the name 'Adi Brahmo Samaj' (Original Brahmo Samaj). The Maharshi-Rabindranath Tagore father-son connection is a common crossover question.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Debendranath Tagore is the 'middle' Brahmo Samaj figure between Roy and Keshab, often skipped; the Rabindranath connection and 'Maharshi' title are tested as identification questions

questions.text: The Rahnumai Mazdayasnan Sabha (1851), a reform organization not often discussed in SSC notes, was associated with which community and which famous person?
options: Muslim community in Bombay; Sir Syed Ahmad Khan
Parsi (Zoroastrian) community; Dadabhai Naoroji was a co-founder
Jewish community in Calcutta; David Sassoon
Christian community in Madras; Bishop Caldwell
questions.explanation: The Rahnumai Mazdayasnan Sabha (Religious Reform Association) was founded in 1851 in Bombay to reform practices within the Parsi (Zoroastrian) community. Its founders included Naoroji Furdonji, S.S. Bengalee, and Dadabhai Naoroji — the same Dadabhai Naoroji famous for the Drain of Wealth theory. The Sabha promoted rationalism in Zoroastrian practice, women's rights and education for Parsi women, and simplification of religious rituals. It published the newspaper Rast Goftar ('Truth-Teller'), edited by Dadabhai Naoroji. This connection — Naoroji as both economic nationalist (Drain of Wealth) and Parsi religious reformer — is a crossover fact that surprises many students.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Parsi reform movements are rarely covered in SSC notes; the Dadabhai Naoroji connection makes this a crossover question across topics

questions.text:
Match the social reformer with their associated organization or work:
1. Raja Ram Mohan Roy   A. Satyashodhak Samaj
2. Jyotirao Phule        B. Young Bengal Movement
3. H.L.V. Derozio        C. Brahmo Samaj
4. Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar  D. Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act
questions.explanation: The correct matches: (1) Raja Ram Mohan Roy → C. Brahmo Samaj (founded 1828 in Bengal); (2) Jyotirao Phule → A. Satyashodhak Samaj (founded 1873 in Pune; anti-caste); (3) H.L.V. Derozio → B. Young Bengal Movement (rationalist movement at Hindu College Calcutta, early 1820s-1831); (4) Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar → D. Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act (1856; his campaign secured the legislation). This match-the-reformer pattern is the single most tested format in 19th-century socio-religious reform questions in SSC CGL.
questions.source: Reformer-to-organization/work matching is the most common question format for this topic; appears in virtually every SSC CGL paper covering Modern History reform movements

questions.text: Who among the following is known as the 'Father of Modern India'?
options: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
Raja Ram Mohan Roy
Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar
Swami Vivekananda
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy (1772-1833) is called the 'Father of Modern India' for initiating India's social and intellectual modernization in the early 19th century — before the railways, before organized nationalism, before most of the institutional changes of the colonial era. He was the first to challenge Sati through both public argument and political advocacy; founded the Brahmo Samaj; pioneered Indian journalism; advocated English education and scientific learning; and took India's concerns to the British Parliament (as Mughal ambassador). He died in Bristol, England in 1833. He is also called the 'Father of Indian Renaissance' and 'Father of Indian Journalism'. Do not confuse: Tilak is 'Father of Indian Unrest' (Valentini Chirol's term); Vivekananda is associated with Ramakrishna Mission.
questions.source: The 'Father of Modern India' title = Ram Mohan Roy is one of the most frequently tested title-person questions in SSC CGL

questions.text: Prarthana Samaj was founded in Bombay in 1867. Which of the following is NOT correctly associated with the Prarthana Samaj?
options: Mahadev Govind Ranade was one of its most prominent leaders
It was inspired by Keshab Chandra Sen's visit to Bombay
It was founded by Jyotirao Phule as an anti-caste organization
R.G. Bhandarkar was associated with it
questions.explanation: The statement that Prarthana Samaj was founded by Jyotirao Phule is incorrect — Phule founded the Satyashodhak Samaj (1873). The Prarthana Samaj was founded by Atmaram Pandurang in 1867. All other options are correct: M.G. Ranade was indeed one of the most prominent leaders of Prarthana Samaj; it was inspired by Keshab Chandra Sen's visit to Bombay; and R.G. Bhandarkar (Sanskrit scholar) was associated with it. The Phule-Prarthana Samaj confusion is the most common mistake students make — Phule's Satyashodhak Samaj was more radical (rejected Brahmin authority entirely) while Prarthana Samaj was more moderate (worked within Hindu tradition).
questions.source: NOT correctly associated / identify the wrong match format is a frequently used question pattern; the Phule-Prarthana Samaj confusion is the most tested trap in Bombay reform questions
